If all else fails and you don't have anything else, a classic that usually works is my accountant's application and he's a nitpicky penny pinching guy who's always driving me crazy and while he's gone I'm running the sale of the century and you gotta buy before he gets back because boy is he gonna be aggravated. That's a classic. It's been used for years and years and years and years and years and years and continues to work. Summation. Every offer, every sales letter, every ad ought to have a summary at the end of that recaps the sale and the longer the copy and the more pages the more important it is. First of all, there's some perverse sons of a gun, skip to the very end to find out what the deal is and so you've got a shot at getting them with this little catch. Second of all, if it's a long deal they're gonna forget part of it by the time they get there so you got them emotionally worked up, they want to buy but they're gonna forget what it is you promised them. So there needs to be a summary. Get some of my ad, I'm very clear about it. Jeff ad, he ends with a box that says let's wrap this up. I've perfected a very unusual mail order money making system, the ideal business gives me over $4,000 a day that I really believe you can learn and use too. I've described everything in my book and you can have your copy for just $19.95, that's absolutely guaranteed. I just summarized the whole ad in a sentence, you know, is what I did. The other ad that runs in here, let's summarize. You can use any of our 15 different promotional methods to find business owners or homeowners interested in saving money on long distance calls. You can let them try our services without paying a cent, even give them four hour service for free and you'll receive a commission on every customer's activity not just the next month but every month of their life. Everything you need to start in this business is provided for just $29.95. So I just summarized the whole ad. If a guy read nothing and went to that, I could get them. Or again it could drive them now back to read the ad. That's why the summary. Yeah, in a sales letter, I'll either do this as a last paragraph or I'll do this as a PS. And I say, oh, well we'll get around to that later. Right there and then we're at the infomercial. Of course. That's what's making sales. But a lot of people in our business think the show is the thing. I see production crews all the time where they've done the entire show and nobody has written word one of the commercial yet. Oh, well we'll get to that. Well that's the whole deal. I mean the pitch is the thing. And preferably it should be an irresistible offer. Two ways to think of that. One, that's John Carlton's thing. John Carlton said, visualize your prospect as a giant sound ambulance sloth half asleep on the couch that almost nothing will move him and you gotta move him. Okay. Um, uh, mild that what I was told early in the direct response to coffee writing I grew up in Ohio, we had bad weather in Ohio. So you imagine the only way they can respond is by sending a letter. They can't call, they're not going to use credit cards, they're going to send in the order. They don't have a stamp. The deadline is tomorrow. There's 60 to snow on the ground. It's snowing like hell. There's an ice storm. And all the news broadcasts are saying if it's all possible stay home, don't go out. What are you going to say to get the guy to go out shovel his driver away, start his car and drive to the post office? Now you got it. And the example of that that I used, I mean I, for about the last, the second last year I was in Ohio, the county was going to school at Murray State University in Burry, Kentucky which is out of maybe seven hours from Akron, Ohio. And, uh, I mean I could see you on weekends, you know. And, uh, so this is a weekend when I was supposed to go to Murray. And I mean it is bad. I mean it is snowing. And it is ice. And it is, I mean it's just shitty out there. And I got this little sports car which you have to put some of that block in the trunk of the car anyway just to keep the thing from sliding all over the place. You know, it only took her three minutes on the phone. Three minutes. It's all in touch. To deliver the irresistible offer. Seven hours I drive through, three, I slide across bridges. All four fenders smashed in by the time I get to Murray. I'm just smacking into bridges. But I got there, three minutes, three in the block. And that's what you have to do. It's got to be that good. Urgent. A lot of people forget that. They build a great offer but there's no real particular reason why you have to respond right now. And that's clearly a mistake because if they don't do it now, the odds are they're not going to do it. How do you get people to believe that? It seems like, maybe because of me and I'm dated, but it seems that in Middle America they're getting it back now. Well, there either has to be a reward or a reward. There's point. There is. It's got to be. There's either got to be a reward and a reason for the reward. You know, it's bad enough just to say we're going to give this to the first hundred callers. Well, that's the old joke in the collectible business. It's a limited edition. It's limited until the one who'll buy it. And that's the limitation. Well, you shouldn't use the fake one. So it's either got to be real. So it's that we're only going to sell a thousand of these this month. We're only going to sell one to every tenth person in the United States in order to keep from having too many people in the same area in the same deal. We're doing it to the first hundred callers from this particular ad. That doesn't mean you can't do it to the first hundred callers for every ad. You have to think your way through how to phrase that. The flip side is to penalize people, which has done a lot in the seminar business, by the way, where you get it. If you sign up by such and such a date, it's X price. If you wait and sign up by such and such a date, it's a higher price. If you wait until the last minute, say at the door, it's an even higher price. That's a penalty approach to this. The other way to create the urgency out of the reward category is by stressing the timeliness of the benefit, you know, in how you say it. You know, you want to respond right now so you can begin losing weight by noon tomorrow. We're sending this to you Federal Express and the guy will be there by 10. You know, that kind of thing. You want to respond right now because it's a 31-day weight loss plan and a beach weather starts in 33-and-a-half days. You know, you really haven't... You're not giving them anything extra. You're not taking anything away from them, but you've made the core benefit of the offer timely. We put a little weather bulletin tag across the commercial during the month of August, September and October. That Christmas is coming and you want to get this right now so you can make extra money for Christmas to create some sense of urgency. I'm not helping you, am I? No, I just... I can tell you're frustrated. I'm frustrated because I've been drawing the length. I've been working with more clients because I go back to that same audience over and over with different offers. And after a while, I mean, a different product related to it has now deployed and has no effect on it. Did I agree with you? Yeah. Otherwise, there's no reason to act? Yeah. You need to see if all else fails, tell them the truth. We want you... I've done that, by the way. I've used that promotion. No, the truth is we want you to respond right now because we know you're busy. Tomorrow there's going to be another 20 pieces in the mail. You know, the tendency is if you don't take care of it right now, this gets buried on a desk. It's buried in a kitchen door, you know, whatever. We want you to take care of it right now and in order to get you to do that, we're willing to give you an extra, you know, whatever. What the hell I did here? I don't even remember what I did. I mean, that's the biggest problem for people to respond when you can put it in. The thing I would say to you is that should be your swipe file focus. It is. It is. I've spent a lot of time on that. Great thing. Secondary reason for response. The idea here is that and it's not always applicable to pure direct response offers. It is always applicable to small business. If you're running any kind of advertising or doing direct mail where there is a, where the primary reason for response is they want your product or service immediately. But you know you're going to reach a lot of people who are not ready for your product or service right now, there could be relief. Then you want a secondary reason for response because you want to capture those names and addresses in order to pursue them later. So let's say we're running an ad for kinder care for a place where people bring them preschool kids. But we know the ads could be read by a lot of people whose kids are old not yet. So we're going to say somewhere, you know, if your kids are old enough yet, at least send for our free report the five ways to raise smarter kids. You know, let's put that in your hands. Well, everybody that sends for the report, what do we know about them? We know they got kids that aren't yet old enough to go to kinder care, but will be someday. And so now we can build a relationship with those people and capture that business when they're ready. And a widget is simply the thing you use to give them an exchange for that response, whatever that free thing is. And when it's a report, a booklet or a book, the title is incredibly important. It drives me nuts when you see ads offering free brochure, free video, free report, and it doesn't have a title, free catalog. This stuff should always have. The thing should be many pitches if you were trying to get money for it. You know, it should be described in an attractive appeal like these guys. Here's the school of VCR repair for free career, a literature. What does that sound like? It sounds like you're going to get an envelope full of brochures, doesn't it? You know, wouldn't it be better, you know, for free career kit, including our book. Ten ways to increase your income. I mean, you know, if you made it sound better, your responses got to go up. Take away selling. To some degree, I try and put some takeaway in everything. That whatever it is we're selling is not available to everybody for one reason or another. And it's not going to be available in this way, shape, or price forever. Think of selling. Yeah, it's like, okay, here it is and now you want it and now maybe you can't have it. In the selling of professional services, such as you do, part of the time, it's critically important. A con book? That's a good thing. Yeah, the, I'm not even sure if we can fit you into the schedule. Not even sure. You know, it's the difference between, in some respects, it's the difference between a thousand dollar or an ISC court and a hooker on the street court. You know, the fact that she's so readily available brings her price down dramatically. And you know, it's also this perversity that people want what they can't have. And if you're too available, you can't be any good. You know, it's why good restaurants make everybody wait for a table even when the place is half empty. You know, because if they see you right away, Jesus' place can't be any good. I forget, did anybody see Fraser last night? You know, the comedy show Fraser? He's taken his dad to this doctor's office, to this new doctor. And the first thing he got excited, the guy's got to be a good doctor because his office is in this particular building, which is a real high-rank building. And then he's got to be great because he recognizes the paintings and the other things. And then the guy is taken in by 10 o'clock. You know, the guy, you know, the fuss of it, he's late for the appointment. Dad's been waiting out for 45 minutes and the receptionist says, well, he hasn't even heard yet, sir. Fraser turns around and says, you know, he's got to be a great doctor. Instead of clocking, he hasn't even heard yet. Well, you know, people do think that way. All might like when someone, and of course it helps when it's true, but like in my stuff, where people now call in and write in and ask for like literature about consulting or information. The cover letter that goes out to them, all of them, includes the coffee stand schedule, which, you know, looks like a night in there, you know. And it's possible, but you're not going to be able to fit you in for a month or two. And certainly you'll see there's a few open dates as soon as you act and, you know, it's like, if you open a new restaurant, you always go rent a bunch of cars and fill the parking lot with cars. Because nobody likes to be the first, I mean, nobody likes to be the first car to drive into an empty parking lot. And you move the cars around all week long. So when they drive by, at least you get the cars in the parking lot and the restaurant will be successful ever more. So there's availability. There's one way that you take away. There's not enough time for me to go around. There's only so many collector planes. There's all, you know, whatever. There's, you know, there's, there's, there's elitism. Diners Club for years did get a mailing, you know, where the headline on the letter was, Diners Club membership is certainly not for everyone. You know, and the letter would start it out, I don't remember, but it would start out with saying, you know, three, it was the exact opposite of the, you know, but we can get you credit pitch. It was three out of four people who applied for a Diners Club, or turned down. And here's, you know, what it means to be a Diners Club member. And it was an elitism pitch. And then everybody wanted a Diners Club card. You know, American Express, there's very little benefit to having a gold card instead of a green card. You pay them money, but there's really very little benefit. They got lots of gold cards because it's a gold card. And so you want to, so you want to create some of that. How would you do something for the team? To be a product. Diet product? I'd probably do that this product is not for everybody and, you know, discredit the obvious person that I'm going to buy anyway. You know, this diet is not for everybody. You know, if you really depending on the product, I mean, pitch against whatever, you know, I mean, you know, if you really think that somewhere there is a magic giant green pill that you can swallow and have weight melt off while you sleep. You know, if you're completely unwilling to learn anything new about behavior and, you know, this thing is not for you. This is for the smart person who wants to take a smart approach to weight loss. You know, I do something like that. In the Larry Pino seminar letter, we have a couple paragraphs about how, you know, if you're the kind of person who plays the lottery, not for entertainment, but actually has a system of numbers that believes you're ever going to win. If you're the kind of person who thinks you're going to solve your problems because a long lost uncle is going to show you that this is not for you. .
